Q1 — A place in memory:
There is this old, damp shed behind my childhood home that I find myself thinking about all the time. It wasn't much to look at, but it always smelled incredible—like fresh rain and heavy, wet earth, even in the middle of a dry summer.

I used to spend hours out there alone, just messing around with the dense mud that would pool near the leaky drainpipe outside. I remember scooping it up and just marveling at how cold and heavy it felt in my palms. I'd sit on an overturned bucket for hours, totally lost in the feeling of the earth yielding to my hands. I'd press my thumbs into the center of a lump of it, pinching the sides up to make little hollow shapes, or I'd roll out long, perfectly even coils and just stack them on top of each other.

I never really kept anything I made; I'd leave them on the wooden bench, and by the next day, they would be dried out and cracked. But there was something deeply grounding about the process. I loved the way I could smooth out a rough, gritty edge just by wetting my fingertips and gliding them over the surface. The whole world would go completely quiet, and it was just me, the smell of the damp earth, and the rhythmic motion of turning a shapeless lump of dirt into something intentional. I don't really know why I keep going back to that memory; it was just a kid playing in the mud, really, but my hands always felt like they knew exactly what they were doing.
Q2 — Something you do:
I have this weird habit of constantly evaluating the weight, balance, and texture of everyday objects—especially cups, mugs, and bowls. If I go to a restaurant or a friend's house, before I even take a sip of water or eat, I find myself picking up the tableware and just... tracing it.

I'll run my thumb around the rim to feel how smooth the lip is, or I'll flip a plate over just to run my fingers along that unpolished, rougher ridge at the very bottom. I am strangely hyper-aware of how the curve of a bowl sits in the palms of my hands, or how the handle of a mug supports the weight of the liquid inside. My friends always laugh and ask if I'm a health inspector checking for chips or dirt, but that's not it at all. I just like feeling the physical structure of it, trying to imagine how it was formed.

Also, if my hands are idle, I *have* to be molding something. A piece of sticky-tack from a poster, a kneaded eraser, even the red wax from a piece of cheese—I will absentmindedly work it between my palms for hours. I'll spend ages rolling it into a perfectly smooth sphere, and then I'll press my thumb directly into the center to make a tiny little hollow vessel. I'll sit there pinching the sides, trying to get the walls perfectly even and symmetrical all the way around, only to crush it into a ball and start all over again. People usually assume it's just nervous fidgeting, but it actually completely relaxes me. It's like my hands just naturally want to be pushing, pulling, and shaping material until it feels perfectly balanced.
Q3 — How does it work:
I'll pick how a muddy patch of earth dries up in the sun after a heavy rain.

The way I see it, when the ground is fully soaked, the earth is completely formless and receptive. It just yields to whatever touches it—a footprint, a stray rock, a fallen branch—and it holds that impression perfectly. It's soft, heavy, and malleable because the water is acting like a lubricant between all the microscopic particles of dirt.

But the fascinating part is how it behaves when the heat of the sun hits it. As the moisture slowly evaporates into the air, the earth actually begins to shrink inward. All those tiny, wet particles pull closer and closer together, binding tight. If the sun is too fierce and the top layer loses its water much faster than the bottom layer, the structural tension gets to be too much, and the surface violently snaps, creating those deep, jagged cracks.

But if it dries slowly and evenly, it hardens into a dense, smooth crust. The heat of the sun essentially bakes it, locking whatever shape or texture was pressed into it permanently in place. I always find myself stopping to look at those dried patches of dirt, marveling at how simply removing the water and applying heat completely transforms the material—taking it from a soft, slippery paste that you can mold easily with your fingers into something hard, rigid, and enduring.
Q4 — A fight you remember:
I got into a remarkably heated argument with an old roommate a few years ago at a local craft market. The surface story was simple: I made us miss our train because I refused to leave a stall, holding us up over a single, kind of ugly, lopsided bowl. She was furious, accusing me of being inconsiderate of her time and stubborn over what she called "a heavy, overpriced piece of dirt."

But the story *behind* the fight was entirely different. The truth was, the moment I picked up that bowl, I experienced this paralyzing, intense fascination that I couldn't put into words. It wasn't a perfectly manufactured sphere; it had a slight, deliberate wobble in the rim. I could physically feel the exact spot where the maker's thumb had pressed just a fraction too hard while the object was spinning.

I was completely transfixed by the tactile history of it. I kept tracing the faint, spiraling grooves left by someone else's wet fingers on the inside walls. I was mesmerized by the gritty, rough texture of the unglazed bottom, and how something that had clearly started as soft, yielding mud had been baked into permanent stone. She thought I was just being difficult to annoy her, but in reality, my hands felt like they had suddenly recognized a language I didn't know how to speak out loud. When she yelled at me to put it down, I felt surprisingly defensive—not of myself, but of the object, and the slow, deliberate process it took to build it from nothing.
Q5 — A sound that stays:
There's a very specific sound that has stuck with me for years: a heavy, wet *thwack* followed by a low, steady whirring hum. I first heard it when I was twelve, walking past an open basement window.

I heard someone repeatedly slamming what sounded like a dense, heavy ball of wet earth onto a hard surface. Then, a small motor kicked in, followed by the slick, continuous sound of wet hands gliding over something spinning fast. I never looked inside, but the sound was hypnotic. It sticks with me because it feels incredibly grounding. The transition from a heavy, chaotic impact to a smooth, centered rhythm feels like the auditory equivalent of bringing order to mud. Whenever my mind is racing, my brain defaults to that exact sound.
Q6 — Body before mind:
I was walking near a riverbank a few years ago when my boot caught on a root, and I took a hard fall into a patch of dense, wet mud. I threw my hands out to brace myself. Before my mind even registered that I had fallen, my hands were already working the earth.

Instead of wiping the mud off in disgust, my fingers instinctively dug in and gathered a thick clump of it. Without me thinking about it, my hands knew the exact pressure needed to squeeze out the excess water. By the time my friends rushed over to see if I was okay, I was just kneeling there, rhythmically kneading the heavy dirt and rolling it into a perfectly smooth, balanced sphere. My brain was still catching up to the fact that I had tripped, but my hands acted like they had been waiting to touch that exact material their whole life.
Q7 — The unworded thing:
It's this deep, wordless craving to shape empty space.

When I cup my hands together, I'm not focused on my fingers—I'm focused on the invisible, hollow volume I'm holding between them. It's the sensation of wanting to find the exact, perfect center of something that is spinning wildly, and just by applying steady, even pressure with wet hands, forcing it to become perfectly still. It's the urge to take something completely formless, heavy, and wet, and slowly pull it upward until it creates a physical boundary that can hold something else. I can't really explain it, but it feels like the exact opposite of destruction. It feels like coaxing permanence out of wet dirt, just by applying the right pressure.
